Page 1

Bearing 315
The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
September - October, 1990

Drunken Charlie Lake Meet

Sunday, September 16

Now that everyone has more or less recovered from APOC, PNWOF and World Cup events, and while the weather is still bright and warm, is an ideal time to orienteer at Drunken Charlie Lake. This is one of our best maps for non-trail orienteering. The courses will use a largely unlogged portion of the map. There will be novice, yellow, green and blue courses.
Registration is 10 to 12 noon. The road will be marked from Cherry Valley Road, about 3 miles north of Duvall.
We still need help with both start and finish. Remember, when you run a course that someone set all those markers, picks them up, and times you on the course. Orienteering is a very volunteer labor intensive sport, and our success depends on your help. Volunteer today!

WORLD CUP 1990 ORIENTEERING

107 competitors from 18 countries (USA, USSR, Sweden, Norway, Finland, Great Britain, Hungary, Ireland, France, Belgium, Switzerland, Czechoslovakia, Denmark, Japan, Canada, Austria, Australia and New Zealand) ran in the World Cup event on Saturday August 18 at Cle Elum.

Winner of the Men's World Cup was Niklas Löwegren of Sweden with a winning time of 77:59 minutes. Havard Tveite of Norway was second with a time of 82:36 and Rolf Vestre of Norway third with 82:40. Mikell Platt was the best US runner, placing 38th with a time of 103:31 minutes.

Winner of the Women's World Cup was Kerstin Haglund of Sweden with a winning time of 72:53 minutes. Ragnhild Bente Andersen of Norway was second at 72:59, and Yvette Hagne of Great Britain was third with 74:27. Peggy Dickison had the best US time, placing 32nd with a time of 111:53 minutes.

The World Cup was run on the new Ros-Elum Ridge map. Ros-Elum Ridge maps with the World Cup courses and winning routes overprinted are available from Harvey Friedmann for $7.50 plus postage.

Club Notes

Board Meeting - July 9
The Board met July 9 at Debbie Newell's. Present were Debbie Newell, Mike Schuh, Ardis Dull, David Soper, Denise Klein, Don Atkinson, Harvey Friedman and Bruce McAlister. Membership at about 150. There were reports on upcoming meets, and meet directors and course designers were appointed for most meets this year. A picnic and short run at St Edwards will be held on August 25.
Ardis reported on the 1990 USOF Convention in Connecticut. Next year's USOF convention will be the last week in June in Pennsylvania. WSOA may write a proposal to host the 1992 USOF Convention.

September 10
Next Board meeting , Monday, September 10, 7:30 pm at David Soper and Denise Klein's. All club members are welcome to attend Board meetings.

Monthly Club Meeting
The regular bi-monthly club meeting will be held on Tuesday, September 18 at 7:00 pm at the University Library at NE 50th and Roosevelt Way. We will review PNWOF and the World Cup and the winning course choices.

Elections
If its November, it must be elections. The November annual meeting and elections will be held on Thursday, November 29 at the University Branch Seattle Public Library at 7:00 p.m. We need a full slate of officers, including a president, as Mike Schuh has indicated he would prefer to take a less active role next year. If you would like to be President - or Vice President - or serve on the Board in some capacity, please let someone on the Board know. In any event, everyone is urged to come to the meeting to review the past and decide the future of Cascade Orienteering.

We will be electing new officers in November. However, in addition to the elected offices, there are a number of important appointed offices, several of which are vacant: Mapping Coordinator, WSOA Rep, Publicity Coordinator, Hotline coordinator, Newsletter Editor (in January). If you are interested either in helping as an assistant, or taking charge in these area, please let any present member of the Board know, and you have a job.

Page 2

Pacific Northwest O'Festival Report

Over 800 competitors ran in the PNWOF meet at Cle Elum. With 15 courses each day, this was the largest non-junior meet ever held in the US. With 30 courses in two days, plus the World Cup, there was opportunity for a few glitches, but all the courses got off; The weather, which had been hot (100+) for weeks turned cool earlier in the week, with rain during the model event on Friday. Saturday, however, the weather was perfect for running, light clouds with temperatures in the 60s. Saturday was run on the new Ros-Elum Ridge map. The new map was challenging. There is more topography and more green than on the Cle-Elum Ridge map and the old mine tailings make for interesting topography. The PNWOF finish was next to the World Cup finish, so PNWOF finishers could watch the World Cup finishers.

The afternoon was taken up with the World Cup presentations. Following dinner, there was contra-dancing in the gymn, and an opportunity for review of the days success (or mistakes) and a hance to meet orienteers from around the world.

Sunday's meet was held on the updated Cle-Elum Ridge map. This is basically an all new map. The meet start was across the river. The first orienteering obstacle was negotiating the log foot bridge. Everything was started and finished under another cloudy and cool day.

Finally, Sunday afternoon saw this intrepid band of orienteers, many of whom had started at Kamloops 16 days earlier scatter to cities and airports around the world.

[Photo Caption: Dave Enger reviews routes with Jim Webster (Calgary) at PNWOF. Scott Donald (Rossland) at right]

Travelling? Check for Discounts

UNITED AIR LINES DISCOUNTS. United Air Lines offers discounts on all flights to "A" meets and some "B" meets. To get a discounted ticket, you or your travel agent simply call (800) 521-4041 and give the name United States Orienteering Federation, our account number "0097D" and then make your discounted reservation.
Discounts are 45% off unrestricted coach fares. Some excursion fares may be lower, but have more restrictions. If so, you get 5% off lowest applicable fare. For example, a 45% discounted round trip to Newark for the Nationals is $503. By comparison, an excursion fare is available for $427 ($449-5%), but is not refundable, and is only available on limited flights.
By comparison, the 45% discounted coach tickets are refundable and exchangeable, and you can travel on most flights. The only restriction is that travel is limited to 3 days before to 3 days after each meet.

MUTUAL TRAVEL - the Official Travel Agency for WSOA. WSOA has an agreement with Mutual Travel that refunds 20% of their commissions received from air travel made by orienteers to WSOA. This covers any travel you book through Mutual and identify as WSOA travel. Our travel agent is Shelli Wehrwein at Mutual, and you must identify youself as WSOA when you call.

Out of State Meets of Interest

US Championships November 3/4
Philmont - October 6/7
The 1990 US Championships are November 3, 4 at Harriman State Park, New York. Several Cascade members are planning to attend. Registration and accommodation information in the next issue of ONA. October 6/7 is the date for a two day "B" meet at Philmont Scout Ranch near Cimarron, New Mexico. Bruce McAlister (821-6862) is looking for someone to share costs.

O - Ringen - Swedish Five Day
Scottish Six Day
We have registration information for the Swedish five day O-Ringen near Stockholm, July 22-26, 1991, and for the Scottish six-day near Dumfriesin the west border country south of Glasgow. Check your vacation schedule and join in two of the premier orienteering events of 1991.

Notes/Comments

Page 4

PRESIDENTIAL SPORTS AWARD

What do the following sports have in common: Aerobic Dance, Archery, Back Packing, Badminton, Baseball, Basketball, Bicycling, Bowling, Canoe-Kayak, Climbing, Equitation, Fencing, Field Hockey, Figure Skating, Fitness Walking, Football, Golf, Gymnastics, Handball, Team Handball, Ice Hockey, Ice Skating, Jogging Judo, Karate, Marathon, ORIENTEERING, Racquetball, Rifle, Roller Skating, Rowing, Rugby, Running, Sailing, Skuba-Skin, Skeet-Trap, Skiing (Alpine), Skiing (Nordic), Soccer, Softball, Sports/Fitness, Squash, Swimming, Table Tennis, Tae Kwon Do, Tennis, Volleyball, Water Skiing, and Weight Training? The answer is that they are the 49 categories of sports or fitness activity which qualify for the PRESIDENTIAL SPORTS AWARD developed by the President's Council on Fitness and Sports, and administered by the Amateur Athletic Union.

You can earn the Award in any one of the 49 sports and fitness activities. Any individual 15 years of age and older is eligible to participate.

To earn the Award: 1) Select your sport or fitness activity (Orienteering, naturally). 2) Keep a record of your participation on the fitness log (see Page 6, copies of the fitness log are acceptable). 3) When you have fulfilled the qualifying standards, send the completed and signed fitness log and $6.00 per award to: Presidential Sports Award, P.O. Box 68207, Indianapolis, IN 46268. Allow six to eight weeks for delivery.

You will receive:
1) A certificate of achievement from President Bush, personalized with your name and suitable for framing.
2) A letter of congratulations from Arnold Schwarzenegger, the Chairman of the Copuncil, suitable for framing.
3) A blazer patch (embroidered emblem) signifying the sport or fitness activity in which you earned your award.
4) A sports bag identification tag, imprinted with the award logo.
5) A "shoe pocket" to hold your valuables and an identification card while you're working out.

The Qualifying Standards for Orienteering are:

  1. Practice orienteering skills for a minimum of 50 hours.
  2. No more than two (2) hours in any day may be credited to the total.
  3. Participate in at least four (4) organized orienteering events and locate all checkpoints within the allotted time.

Bruce McAlister has more information if you are interested.

Cascade Chatter

compiled by Harvey Friedman

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 4 September 1990. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Mark Hartinger, Francisco & Ana Lazo, Doug & Katie Sprugel. If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you want to get your handsome membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs' functions. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your newsletter to be late (or lost).

Now on to the fun stuff...Doug & Katie Sprugel are proud new parents of Ben, born shortly before the World Cup & PNWOF excitement....Before that, Pat Jarvis Albright wnet when A to Z when she married Peter Zurcher from the Rocky Mountain O' Club ( Colorado). The wedding was in Mt. Vernon and the reception party at the Sons of Norway lodge up there. Pat now goes by Pat Albright-Zurcher but she goes to Colorado to live with Peter and will only visit us here occasionally....Goran & Berit Larsson and their children, Robert & Fredrik, along with her parents, Elsie & Karl-Ake Eriksson, all attended PNWOF. As meet director, I was too busy to chat much, but I hope all their old friends made them feel welcome again....Speaking of making folks feel welcome, Bruce McAlister, had a Brit, a Dane, 2 Swedes, 3 Czechs, and a couple of Americans staying with him after PNWOF. Robert & Sandy Miller stored bicycles for some New Zealanders. Dave Tallent had Austrian visitors, and I'm sure there are others I missed. There were plenty of foreign visitors to host from 20 different countries....Orienteering is a sport of navigation, true, but it is more than just going thru the woods with a map; making new friends and renewing acquaintances with familiar faces are also important factors making O' the wonderful activity that it is. Families meeting families make for travel experiences to other countries that might be otherwise too costly to afford. Contact me if you need more information....If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column since I have such a bad memory.

Clue Sheets Move into the Computer Age

Have you ever had a control description sheet (commonly known as the clue sheet) with numbers and symbols that took more time to decipher than finding the next control? Cascade has moved into the computer age with two clue sheet programs. "Clue Sheet" by David Dana is a Macintosh program which was reviewed in ONA for August. "Condes" is a program developed by Finn Arildsen of the IOF computer group. It is an IBM compatible program for either dot-matrix or Postscript laser printers. The clue sheet at right is from the August 25 St Edwards meet, and was prepared using the "Condes" program. If you are a course designer, "Condes" is available from Bruce McAlister and "Clue Sheet" is available from Katie Sprugel. Both programs are simple to use, cover the entire set of IOF symbols, and allow typed descriptions if desired.

The first line lists the course class designation, the shortest practical length and the optimum climb. The last line shows the distance and markings, if any, to the finish. Distances are in meters.

The individual control descriptions have 8 columns. Column 1 shows the number of the control as it appears on the map. Column 2 shows the number on the control marker itself.

The remaining columns contain symbols from the list to the right. Column 4 is the most important and shows on which type of feature the control is located. If there are several similar features in the vicinity, column 3 is used to show which one. Columns 5 and 6 are used to further describe the feature. Column 5 may show a second feature present, or, if the control is at ajunction or crossing of dissimilar features, Column 6 may be used for the second feature. Column 6 is usually used to show the feature's size (in meters). Column 7 shows on which part of the feature the marker is located, and Column 8 is used for non-navigational information (such as water) about the control.

[Control Description Sheet Example for St Edwards August 25]
Long | 3.2 Km | 30 m

1 | H1 | Path (curved) | | | | | |
2 | H2 | Boulder | | | | | |
3 | H3 | Between | Boulder (0.5m) | | | | |
4 | H4 | Boulder | | | | | |
5 | H5 | Path | | | | | |
6 | H6 | Thicket | Open land (inside corner) | | | | |
7 | H7 | Path | | | | | |
8 | H8 | Crossing | Path / Path | Man made | | |

block_index.text: Bearing 315 The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club September - October, 1990

Now that everyone has more or less recovered from APOC, PNWOF and World Cup events, and while the weather is still bright and warm, is an ideal time to orienteer at Drunken Charlie Lake. This is one of our best maps for non-trail orienteering. The courses will use a largely unlogged portion of the map. There will be novice, yellow, green and blue courses. Registration is 10 to 12 noon. The road will be marked from Cherry Valley Road, about 3 miles north of Duvall. We still need help with both start and finish. Remember, when you run a course that someone set all those markers, picks them up, and times you on the course. Orienteering is a very volunteer labor intensive sport, and our success depends on your help. Volunteer today!

107 competitors from 18 countries (USA, USSR, Sweden, Norway, Finland, Great Britain, Hungary, Ireland, France, Belgium, Switzerland, Czechoslovakia, Denmark, Japan, Canada, Austria, Australia and New Zealand) ran in the World Cup event on Saturday August 18 at Cle Elum.

Winner of the Men's World Cup was Niklas Löwegren of Sweden with a winning time of 77:59 minutes. Havard Tveite of Norway was second with a time of 82:36 and Rolf Vestre of Norway third with 82:40. Mikell Platt was the best US runner, placing 38th with a time of 103:31 minutes.

Winner of the Women's World Cup was Kerstin Haglund of Sweden with a winning time of 72:53 minutes. Ragnhild Bente Andersen of Norway was second at 72:59, and Yvette Hagne of Great Britain was third with 74:27. Peggy Dickison had the best US time, placing 32nd with a time of 111:53 minutes.
The World Cup was run on the new Ros-Elum Ridge map. Ros-Elum Ridge maps with the World Cup courses and winning routes overprinted are available from Harvey Friedmann for $7.50 plus postage.

Board Meeting - July 9 The Board met July 9 at Debbie Newell's. Present were Debbie Newell, Mike Schuh, Ardis Dull, David Soper, Denise Klein, Don Atkinson, Harvey Friedman and Bruce McAlister. Membership at about 150. There were reports on upcoming meets, and meet directors and course designers were appointed for most meets this year. A picnic and short run at St Edwards will be held on August 25. Ardis reported on the 1990 USOF Convention in Connecticut. Next year's USOF convention will be the last week in June in Pennsylvania. WSOA may write a proposal to host the 1992 USOF Convention.
September 10 Next Board meeting , Monday, September 10, 7:30 pm at David Soper and Denise Klein's. All club members are welcome to attend Board meetings.

Monthly Club Meeting The regular bi-monthly club meeting will be held on Tuesday, September 18 at 7:00 pm at the University Library at NE 50th and Roosevelt Way. We will review PNWOF and the World Cup and the winning course choices.

Elections If its November, it must be elections. The November annual meeting and elections will be held on Thursday, November 29 at the University Branch Seattle Public Library at 7:00 p.m. We need a full slate of officers, including a president, as Mike Schuh has indicated he would prefer to take a less active role next year. If you would like to be President - or Vice President - or serve on the Board in some capacity, please let someone on the Board know. In any event, everyone is urged to come to the meeting to review the past and decide the future of Cascade Orienteering.

We will be electing new officers in November. However, in addition to the elected offices, there are a number of important appointed offices, several of which are vacant: Mapping Coordinator, WSOA Rep, Publicity Coordinator, Hotline coordinator, Newsletter Editor (in January). If you are interested either in helping as an assistant, or taking charge in these area, please let any present member of the Board know, and you have a job.

Over 800 competitors ran in the PNWOF meet at Cle Elum. With 15 courses each day, this was the largest non-junior meet ever held in the US. With 30 courses in two days, plus the World Cup, there was opportunity for a few glitches, but all the courses got off; The weather, which had been hot (100+) for weeks turned cool earlier in the week, with rain during the model event on Friday. Saturday, however, the weather was perfect for running, light clouds with temperatures in the 60s. Saturday was run on the new Ros-Elum Ridge map. The new map was challenging. There is more topography and more green than on the Cle-Elum Ridge map and the old mine tailings make for interesting topography. The PNWOF finish was next to the World Cup finish, so PNWOF finishers could watch the World Cup finishers.

The afternoon was taken up with the World Cup presentations. Following dinner, there was contra-dancing in the gymn, and an opportunity for review of the days success (or mistakes) and a hance to meet orienteers from around the world.

Sunday's meet was held on the updated Cle-Elum Ridge map. This is basically an all new map. The meet start was across the river. The first orienteering obstacle was negotiating the log foot bridge. Everything was started and finished under another cloudy and cool day.
Finally, Sunday afternoon saw this intrepid band of orienteers, many of whom had started at Kamloops 16 days earlier scatter to cities and airports around the world.
[Photo Caption: Dave Enger reviews routes with Jim Webster (Calgary) at PNWOF. Scott Donald (Rossland) at right]

UNITED AIR LINES DISCOUNTS. United Air Lines offers discounts on all flights to "A" meets and some "B" meets. To get a discounted ticket, you or your travel agent simply call (800) 521-4041 and give the name United States Orienteering Federation, our account number "0097D" and then make your discounted reservation. Discounts are 45% off unrestricted coach fares. Some excursion fares may be lower, but have more restrictions. If so, you get 5% off lowest applicable fare. For example, a 45% discounted round trip to Newark for the Nationals is $503. By comparison, an excursion fare is available for $427 ($449-5%), but is not refundable, and is only available on limited flights. By comparison, the 45% discounted coach tickets are refundable and exchangeable, and you can travel on most flights. The only restriction is that travel is limited to 3 days before to 3 days after each meet.

MUTUAL TRAVEL - the Official Travel Agency for WSOA. WSOA has an agreement with Mutual Travel that refunds 20% of their commissions received from air travel made by orienteers to WSOA. This covers any travel you book through Mutual and identify as WSOA travel. Our travel agent is Shelli Wehrwein at Mutual, and you must identify youself as WSOA when you call.
Out of State Meets of Interest

US Championships November 3/4 Philmont - October 6/7 The 1990 US Championships are November 3, 4 at Harriman State Park, New York. Several Cascade members are planning to attend. Registration and accommodation information in the next issue of ONA. October 6/7 is the date for a two day "B" meet at Philmont Scout Ranch near Cimarron, New Mexico. Bruce McAlister (821-6862) is looking for someone to share costs.

O - Ringen - Swedish Five Day Scottish Six Day We have registration information for the Swedish five day O-Ringen near Stockholm, July 22-26, 1991, and for the Scottish six-day near Dumfriesin the west border country south of Glasgow. Check your vacation schedule and join in two of the premier orienteering events of 1991.

I hereby affirm that I have fulfilled all of the requirements for the Presidential Sports Award in . Name of Sport Signature I obtained my fitness log at/from

Name: [ ] Street/P.O. Box: [ ] Apt: [ ] City: [ ] State: [ ] Zip Code: [ ] - [ ]
Send additional Fitness Log Yes No Presidential Sports Award, P.O. Box 68207, Indianapolis, IN 46268

DID YOU REMEMBER TO: Complete and sign Fitness Log? Print name and address clearly? Enclose $6.00 per award? (United States currency -- Make check/money order payable to "AAU/Presidential Sports Award")

What do the following sports have in common: Aerobic Dance, Archery, Back Packing, Badminton, Baseball, Basketball, Bicycling, Bowling, Canoe-Kayak, Climbing, Equitation, Fencing, Field Hockey, Figure Skating, Fitness Walking, Football, Golf, Gymnastics, Handball, Team Handball, Ice Hockey, Ice Skating, Jogging Judo, Karate, Marathon, ORIENTEERING, Racquetball, Rifle, Roller Skating, Rowing, Rugby, Running, Sailing, Skuba-Skin, Skeet-Trap, Skiing (Alpine), Skiing (Nordic), Soccer, Softball, Sports/Fitness, Squash, Swimming, Table Tennis, Tae Kwon Do, Tennis, Volleyball, Water Skiing, and Weight Training? The answer is that they are the 49 categories of sports or fitness activity which qualify for the PRESIDENTIAL SPORTS AWARD developed by the President's Council on Fitness and Sports, and administered by the Amateur Athletic Union.
You can earn the Award in any one of the 49 sports and fitness activities. Any individual 15 years of age and older is eligible to participate.

To earn the Award: 1) Select your sport or fitness activity (Orienteering, naturally). 2) Keep a record of your participation on the fitness log (see Page 6, copies of the fitness log are acceptable). 3) When you have fulfilled the qualifying standards, send the completed and signed fitness log and $6.00 per award to: Presidential Sports Award, P.O. Box 68207, Indianapolis, IN 46268. Allow six to eight weeks for delivery.

You will receive: 1) A certificate of achievement from President Bush, personalized with your name and suitable for framing. 2) A letter of congratulations from Arnold Schwarzenegger, the Chairman of the Copuncil, suitable for framing. 3) A blazer patch (embroidered emblem) signifying the sport or fitness activity in which you earned your award. 4) A sports bag identification tag, imprinted with the award logo. 5) A "shoe pocket" to hold your valuables and an identification card while you're working out.
The Qualifying Standards for Orienteering are:
Practice orienteering skills for a minimum of 50 hours.
No more than two (2) hours in any day may be credited to the total.
Participate in at least four (4) organized orienteering events and locate all checkpoints within the allotted time.
Bruce McAlister has more information if you are interested.

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 4 September 1990. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Mark Hartinger, Francisco & Ana Lazo, Doug & Katie Sprugel. If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you want to get your handsome membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs' functions. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your newsletter to be late (or lost).

Now on to the fun stuff...Doug & Katie Sprugel are proud new parents of Ben, born shortly before the World Cup & PNWOF excitement....Before that, Pat Jarvis Albright wnet when A to Z when she married Peter Zurcher from the Rocky Mountain O' Club ( Colorado). The wedding was in Mt. Vernon and the reception party at the Sons of Norway lodge up there. Pat now goes by Pat Albright-Zurcher but she goes to Colorado to live with Peter and will only visit us here occasionally....Goran & Berit Larsson and their children, Robert & Fredrik, along with her parents, Elsie & Karl-Ake Eriksson, all attended PNWOF. As meet director, I was too busy to chat much, but I hope all their old friends made them feel welcome again....Speaking of making folks feel welcome, Bruce McAlister, had a Brit, a Dane, 2 Swedes, 3 Czechs, and a couple of Americans staying with him after PNWOF. Robert & Sandy Miller stored bicycles for some New Zealanders. Dave Tallent had Austrian visitors, and I'm sure there are others I missed. There were plenty of foreign visitors to host from 20 different countries....Orienteering is a sport of navigation, true, but it is more than just going thru the woods with a map; making new friends and renewing acquaintances with familiar faces are also important factors making O' the wonderful activity that it is. Families meeting families make for travel experiences to other countries that might be otherwise too costly to afford. Contact me if you need more information....If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column since I have such a bad memory.
Christine Brown, Esk Valley O C/AUS

Course 2 (score-o, 16 controls, 150 points possible, 1 pt./min. deduction over 60 minutes)
Clue Sheets Move into the Computer Age

Have you ever had a control description sheet (commonly known as the clue sheet) with numbers and symbols that took more time to decipher than finding the next control? Cascade has moved into the computer age with two clue sheet programs. "Clue Sheet" by David Dana is a Macintosh program which was reviewed in ONA for August. "Condes" is a program developed by Finn Arildsen of the IOF computer group. It is an IBM compatible program for either dot-matrix or Postscript laser printers. The clue sheet at right is from the August 25 St Edwards meet, and was prepared using the "Condes" program. If you are a course designer, "Condes" is available from Bruce McAlister and "Clue Sheet" is available from Katie Sprugel. Both programs are simple to use, cover the entire set of IOF symbols, and allow typed descriptions if desired.
The first line lists the course class designation, the shortest practical length and the optimum climb. The last line shows the distance and markings, if any, to the finish. Distances are in meters.
The individual control descriptions have 8 columns. Column 1 shows the number of the control as it appears on the map. Column 2 shows the number on the control marker itself.

The remaining columns contain symbols from the list to the right. Column 4 is the most important and shows on which type of feature the control is located. If there are several similar features in the vicinity, column 3 is used to show which one. Columns 5 and 6 are used to further describe the feature. Column 5 may show a second feature present, or, if the control is at ajunction or crossing of dissimilar features, Column 6 may be used for the second feature. Column 6 is usually used to show the feature's size (in meters). Column 7 shows on which part of the feature the marker is located, and Column 8 is used for non-navigational information (such as water) about the control.
[Control Description Sheet Example for St Edwards August 25] Long | 3.2 Km | 30 m
